title: Why Bohr's Colleagues Became Acolytes — And Physics Paid the Price

source.original: What Is Quantum Mechanics Really Telling Us? — David Deutsch & Brian Greene | World Science Festival

body_html:
Niels Bohr occupies a singular position in the history of quantum mechanics — not because he was wrong in trying to understand the theory, but because his charisma turned his provisional groping toward understanding into dogma. Deutsch, drawing on insights from his supervisor Bryce DeWitt and his mentor Dennis Sciama, describes how Bohr's influence created what amounts to a cultural pathology in physics: an "induced cringe" that trained students not to ask foundational questions.

The mechanism was subtle. Bohr himself never invoked wave function collapse, and he insisted that quantum theory was the fullest possible description of the world — a position that, ironically, is precisely what Everett also claimed. But Bohr did not follow the logic to its conclusion. His colleagues, however, took his cautious philosophical hedging as the essence of the new physics and turned it into a prohibition: you must not ask what is really happening behind the formalism.

The cultural consequences were enormous. Students who entered physics to understand the world were taught that such understanding was impossible or meaningless. They learned to substitute talk of abstract mathematical objects — wave functions, Hilbert spaces — for talk of physical reality. Each student believed they were alone in finding this unsatisfying, when in fact the discomfort was nearly universal. The result was a community-wide adoption of instrumentalism that Deutsch sees as a betrayal of the purpose of physics.

Deutsch also notes that the broader intellectual climate — Einstein rewriting space and time, Wittgenstein reducing philosophy to language games, modernism transforming art — may have made physicists eager to claim their own conceptual revolution. Quantum mechanics, recast as a prohibition on asking questions about reality, was ready-made for that role. Whatever Bohr's personal motivations, the long shadow he cast over physics is, in Deutsch's view, one of the great intellectual tragedies of the twentieth century.
